content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 2 — SOPHIA Consensus Translation
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **Sources:** EMET (Literal), LOGOS (Formal), PNEUMA (Dynamic)
**Hebrew Base:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 197–198
**LXX Reference:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 144–145
**Disagreement Log:** `disagreements/DEU_002.md`

---

## Council Notes

Chapter 2 narrates the wilderness circuit around Edom and Moab, the divine commands not to encroach on Edomite, Moabite, or Ammonite territory, the passage of thirty-eight years until the war generation died, and the first conquest — Sihon of Heshbon. The chapter contains the remarkable anthropological parentheses (vv.10–12, 20–23) noting pre-Israelite giant peoples (Emim, Zamzummim, Horites, Avvim, Caphtorim). These are preserved in full.

Key rulings: "sons of Esau" for *bney Esav* (established genealogical term throughout); "sons of Lot" for *bney Lot* (parallel structure); "devote to destruction" for *charam* (consistent cherem terminology); "words of peace" preserved as a formulaic diplomatic phrase; Hebrew *Yam Suph* rendered "Red Sea" per project convention.

The three translators showed substantial agreement on this chapter's geography and narrative. Main divergences involve: "Red Sea" vs. "Reed Sea," treatment of "sons of" vs. "children of" genealogical formulas, "contend/provoke/harass" verbs, and the hardening of Sihon's heart.

---

## Consensus Translation

1 "Then we turned and set out into the wilderness by the way of the Red Sea, as the LORD had spoken to me, and we went around Mount Seir many days.

2 And the LORD said to me:

3 'You have been going around this mountain long enough. Turn northward.

4 And command the people, saying: You are about to pass through the territory of your brothers, the sons of Esau who dwell in Seir, and they will be afraid of you — so be very careful.

5 Do not provoke them, for I will not give you any of their land, not so much as a foot's tread, because I have given Mount Seir to Esau as a possession.

6 You shall purchase food from them with silver so that you may eat, and you shall also buy water from them with silver so that you may drink.

7 For the LORD your God has blessed you in all the work of your hands. He has known your walking through this great wilderness; these forty years the LORD your God has been with you — you have lacked nothing.'

8 So we passed by from our brothers, the sons of Esau who dwell in Seir, away from the Arabah road, from Elath and from Ezion-geber. And we turned and passed by the way of the wilderness of Moab.

9 And the LORD said to me: 'Do not harass Moab and do not provoke war against them, for I will not give you any of their land as a possession, because I have given Ar to the sons of Lot as a possession.

10 The Emim formerly dwelt there — a people great and numerous and tall as the Anakim.

11 They too were reckoned as Rephaim, like the Anakim, but the Moabites called them Emim.

12 And in Seir the Horites formerly dwelt, but the sons of Esau dispossessed them and destroyed them from before them and settled in their place — just as Israel did to the land of his possession which the LORD gave to them.

13 Now rise up and cross the Wadi Zered.' And we crossed the Wadi Zered.

14 And the days we traveled from Kadesh-barnea until we crossed the Wadi Zered were thirty-eight years, until the entire generation of the men of war had perished from the midst of the camp, as the LORD had sworn to them.

15 Moreover, the hand of the LORD was against them, to root them out from the midst of the camp, until they had perished.

16 And it came about when all the men of war had finished dying from among the people,

17 that the LORD spoke to me, saying:

18 'Today you are crossing the border of Moab, past Ar.

19 And when you approach opposite the sons of Ammon, do not harass them and do not provoke war against them, for I will not give you any of the land of the sons of Ammon as a possession, because I have given it to the sons of Lot as a possession.'

20 (That land also is reckoned as the land of the Rephaim. Rephaim formerly dwelt there, but the Ammonites called them Zamzummim —

21 a people great and numerous and tall as the Anakim; and the LORD destroyed them before them, and they dispossessed them and settled in their place —

22 just as he did for the sons of Esau who dwell in Seir, when he destroyed the Horites before them, and they dispossessed them and settled in their place to this day.

23 And the Avvim who dwelt in villages as far as Gaza — the Caphtorim who came from Caphtor destroyed them and settled in their place.)

24 'Rise up, set out, and cross the Wadi Arnon. See — I have given into your hand Sihon the Amorite king of Heshbon, and his land. Begin to take possession of it, and engage him in battle.

25 This very day I will begin to put the dread of you and the fear of you upon the peoples under all the heavens, who shall hear the report of you and shall tremble and writhe because of you.'

26 So I sent messengers from the wilderness of Kedemoth to Sihon king of Heshbon with words of peace, saying:

27 'Let me pass through your land; I will go only along the road — I will not turn aside to the right or to the left.

28 Food for silver you shall sell me so that I may eat, and water for silver you shall give me so that I may drink. Only let me pass through on foot —

29 as the sons of Esau who dwell in Seir did for me, and the Moabites who dwell in Ar — until I cross the Jordan into the land that the LORD our God is giving to us.'

30 But Sihon king of Heshbon was not willing to let us pass through, because the LORD your God hardened his spirit and made his heart obstinate, so that he might give him into your hand, as at this day.

31 And the LORD said to me: 'See — I have begun to give Sihon and his land before you. Begin to take possession, to inherit his land.'

32 And Sihon came out against us — he and all his people — to battle at Jahaz.

33 And the LORD our God gave him before us, and we struck him down, along with his sons and all his people.

34 And we captured all his cities at that time and devoted them to destruction — every city: men, women, and children. We left no survivor.

35 Only the livestock we plundered for ourselves, along with the spoil of the cities that we captured.

36 From Aroer, which is on the edge of the Wadi Arnon, and from the city that is in the wadi, as far as Gilead — there was not a city too strong for us. The LORD our God gave them all before us.

37 Only to the land of the sons of Ammon you did not draw near — all the banks of the Wadi Jabbok, and the cities of the hill country, and all that the LORD our God had forbidden to us.

---

## Verse-by-Verse Ruling Notes

**V.1 — "Reed Sea" vs. "Red Sea":** EMET: "Reed Sea." LOGOS/PNEUMA: "Red Sea." Project convention: "Red Sea." SOPHIA follows LOGOS/PNEUMA.

**V.1 — "went around" vs. "circled around" vs. "wandered":** EMET: "went around Mount Se'ir many days." LOGOS: "circled around Mount Seir many days." PNEUMA: "wandered for a long time around the hill country of Seir." The Hebrew *va-nasov* is "we went around/circled." "Went around" (EMET) is plain and accurate; "wandered" (PNEUMA) slightly over-interprets. SOPHIA: "went around Mount Seir many days."

**V.4 — "sons of Esau" vs. "children of Esau" vs. "descendants of Esau":** EMET: "sons of Esav." LOGOS: "children of Esau." PNEUMA: "descendants of Esau." SOPHIA: "sons of Esau" — the genealogical *bney* formula is consistently rendered "sons of" throughout this project (cf. "sons of Israel," "sons of Lot," "sons of Ammon").

**V.5 — "provoke" vs. "contend with" vs. "provoke to war":** EMET: "Do not provoke them." LOGOS: "Do not contend with them." PNEUMA: "Do not provoke them to war." The Hebrew *titgar bam* means "do not stir up strife with them." SOPHIA: "Do not provoke them" (EMET/PNEUMA without the "to war" expansion that is not yet present in this verse — the full phrase appears at v.9).

**V.5 — "foot's tread" vs. "footstep" vs. "single foot of their land":** EMET: "a foot's tread." LOGOS: "a footstep." PNEUMA: "so much as a single foot of their land." The Hebrew *ad midrak kaf-ragel* — "as far as the tread of a foot's sole." SOPHIA: "a foot's tread" (EMET) — most faithful to the idiom.

**V.7 — "known your walking" vs. "knows your wandering" vs. "watched over your journey":** EMET: "He has known your walking." LOGOS: "He knows your wandering." PNEUMA: "He has watched over your journey." The Hebrew *yada' lekhtekha* — "he has known your walking." SOPHIA: "He has known your walking" (EMET) — the intimate connotation of *yada'* (covenantal knowing) is best preserved without smoothing to "watched over."

**V.9 — "harass" vs. "trouble" / "provoke war" vs. "contend in battle":** EMET: "Do not harass Mo'av and do not provoke war against them." LOGOS: "Do not harass Moab, and do not contend with them in battle." PNEUMA: "Do not trouble Moab or provoke them to war." SOPHIA: "Do not harass Moab and do not provoke war against them" (EMET) — "harass" and "provoke war" precisely capture the two distinct Hebrew verbs (*al-tatsar* and *al-titgar*).

**V.12 — "Horites" vs. "Chorim":** EMET: "Chorim." LOGOS/PNEUMA: "Horites." SOPHIA adopts "Horites" — the English conventional form.

**V.15 — "root them out" vs. "destroy them" vs. "confound them":** EMET: "to destroy them from the midst of the camp." LOGOS: "to confound them from the midst of the camp." PNEUMA: "to root them out from the camp." The Hebrew *le-hummam* (Hiphil of *hamam*) means to throw into confusion/destroy. SOPHIA: "to root them out from the midst of the camp" (PNEUMA) — captures the thoroughness of divine action.

**V.18 — "Today you are passing over" vs. "Today you are to cross" vs. "Today you are crossing":** EMET: "You are passing over today the border of Mo'av." LOGOS: "Today you are to cross the border of Moab at Ar." PNEUMA: "Today you are to cross the border of Moab, past Ar." SOPHIA: "Today you are crossing the border of Moab, past Ar" — present continuous reflects the immediacy of the Hebrew participle *over*.

**V.24 — "engage him in battle" vs. "provoke war against him" vs. "wage war against him":** EMET: "provoke war against him." LOGOS: "contend with him in battle." PNEUMA: "wage war against him." SOPHIA: "engage him in battle" — a middle ground that is clear and precise.

**V.25 — "tremble and writhe" vs. "tremble and writhe in anguish":** EMET: "shall tremble and writhe." LOGOS: "shall tremble and writhe." PNEUMA: "will tremble and writhe in anguish." The Hebrew *yir'gu ve-chalu* = "they will tremble and writhe." SOPHIA: "shall tremble and writhe" (EMET/LOGOS) — PNEUMA's "in anguish" is not in the MT.

**V.30 — "hardened his spirit and made his heart stubborn/obstinate":** EMET: "hardened his spirit and made his heart stubborn." LOGOS: "hardened his spirit and made his heart obstinate." PNEUMA: "made him stubborn and hardened his heart." The Hebrew has *hikshah...rucho* (hardened his spirit) and *imets...levavo* (made firm his heart). SOPHIA: "hardened his spirit and made his heart obstinate" (LOGOS) — two distinct Hebrew verbs with two distinct nouns, both preserved. PNEUMA collapses the two-part structure.

**V.34 — "devoted to destruction" vs. "completely destroyed" / "men, women, and children" vs. "little ones":** EMET: "devoted to destruction every city — men and women and little ones." LOGOS: "devoted them to destruction — men, women, and children." PNEUMA: "completely destroyed every city — men, women, and children." SOPHIA: "devoted them to destruction — every city: men, women, and children." "Devoted to destruction" preserves the *cherem* theological term. "Children" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is adopted over EMET's "little ones" as clearer English, though both are acceptable translations of *tap*.

**V.37 — "sons of Ammon" vs. "children of Ammon" / "what the LORD had forbidden" vs. "commanded us":** EMET: "the land of the sons of Ammon...all that the LORD our God commanded us." LOGOS: "the land of the children of Ammon...whatever the LORD our God had forbidden to us." PNEUMA: "the land of the Ammonites...anywhere else the LORD our God had forbidden." SOPHIA: "sons of Ammon" per convention; "all that the LORD our God had forbidden to us" — "forbidden" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is more accurate than "commanded" for *tsivvah* in this negative/restrictive context.

---

## Textual Notes for the Consensus Record

**Vv.10–12, 20–23 — Anthropological parentheses:** These remarkable asides (Emim, Rephaim, Horites, Zamzummim, Avvim, Caphtorim) follow a theological pattern: God has displaced earlier peoples to establish the nations; the same divine logic applies to Israel's inheritance. All three translators preserve them in full. The LXX of Vaticanus preserves these sections as well, with slight spelling variation in the ethnonyms.

**V.20 — Zamzummim:** All three translators preserve "Zamzummim" untranslated. The LXX reads *Ζομζομμίν*. The MT term may be onomatopoeia.

**V.30 — Divine hardening:** The two-verb, two-noun structure of the hardening (*ruach* hardened, *lev* made obstinate) parallels the Pharaoh narratives of Exodus. The LXX Vaticanus renders *ruach* as *pneuma* — the "spirit" of Sihon hardened by God. This is one of several places where the Septuagint's choice of *pneuma* for *ruach* has significant theological implications.

**V.37 — Ammon excepted:** The narrative establishes both the scope and limits of the conquest. The boundary at Jabbok (Ammon's border) is honored. This closing limit is structurally significant.
